coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated MIL-100(Fe) composites for dye degradation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Mukherjee, Debarati
Das, Pradip
Kundu, Sukanya
Mohan Kundu, Lal
Mandal, Bishnupada
coredata.dc:description: The present work outlines the synthesis of a visible light responsive photocatalyst by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQD) in an iron-based metal organic framework (MOF) (i.e., MIL-100(Fe). The composite was synthesized via one-pot solvothermal approach and applied for degradation of Congo red (CR) dye from contaminated water. The composite was characterized in terms of structure, functionality, morphology, and optical properties. Noteworthy, the composite showed significant improvement in the photocatalytic activity in comparison with nascent MIL-100(Fe). Incorporation of GQD in the MOF framework resulted in increased surface area and pore volume and reduced PL intensity. MIL-100/GQD showed maximum dye degradation of 93.8% within 5 min of contact time using photocatalyst dose of 0.025 g/L. The incorporation of GQD resulted in increased charge separation between holes and electrons compared to pre MIL-100 (Fe) as evident from the PL spectra. The Mott-Schottky plot and the trapping experiment demonstrated that the active species like h+ and ̇O2− were responsible for degradation of CR dye. The photocatalyst exhibited appreciable reusability even after four cycles of reuse.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Degradation
MOFs
Graphene quantum dot
Congo red

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of contact time on degradation of CR
Effect of GQD on degradation of CR
Effect of photocatalyst concentration on degradation of CR
Effect of dye concentration on degradation of CR
Photocatalytic mechanism for degradation of CR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the characterization facilities provided by the facility of the central instrument (CIF), Department of Chemistry, and Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Guwahati.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) FTIR, (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm and (d) pore size distribution plot of MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100(Fe)/GQD.

XPS survey spectra of (a) MIL-100(Fe) and (b) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD; High resolution Fe2p spectra of (c) MIL-100(Fe) and (d) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD; High resolution O1s spectra of (e) MIL-100(Fe) and (f) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD; High resolution C1s spectra of (g) MIL-100(Fe) and (h) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD.
FESEM images of (a) GO, (c) MIL-100(Fe),(f) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD and (g) high magnified images of MIL-100(Fe)/GQD and FETEM images of (b) GO, (d) MIL-100(Fe), (e) GQD and (h) MIL-100(Fe)/GQD.
(a) UV–Vis DRS spectrum of the MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100(Fe)/GQD, (b) Tauc plot of the MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100(Fe)/GQD.
(a) Plot of percentage removal of Congo red vs time and (b) Plot of C/Co vs time.
(a) Effect of GQD on percentage degradation of CR; Plot of percentage removal of Congo-red by changing the (b) pH; (c) dosage, (d) initial dye concentration.
(a) Plot of percentage removal of Congo-red in the presence of scavengers, (b) Mott Schottky plot and (c) Conduction band and valence band position of MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100(Fe)/GQD.
Reusability test of prepared composite photocatalyst.
Removal efficiency of various modified metal organic frameworks.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots decorated MIL-100(Fe) composites for dye degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Separation Science Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati 781039, Assam, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Separation Science Laboratory, Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati 781039, Assam, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: All the authors contribute equally as first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work outlines the synthesis of a visible light responsive photocatalyst by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQD) in an iron-based metal organic framework (MOF) (i.e., MIL-100(Fe). The composite was synthesized via one-pot solvothermal approach and applied for degradation of Congo red (CR) dye from contaminated water. The composite was characterized in terms of structure, functionality, morphology, and optical properties. Noteworthy, the composite showed significant improvement in the photocatalytic activity in comparison with nascent MIL-100(Fe). Incorporation of GQD in the MOF framework resulted in increased surface area and pore volume and reduced PL intensity. MIL-100/GQD showed maximum dye degradation of 93.8% within 5 min of contact time using photocatalyst dose of 0.025 g/L. The incorporation of GQD resulted in increased charge separation between holes and electrons compared to pre MIL-100 (Fe) as evident from the PL spectra. The Mott-Schottky plot and the trapping experiment demonstrated that the active species like h+ and ̇O2− were responsible for degradation of CR dye. The photocatalyst exhibited appreciable reusability even after four cycles of reuse.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: There have been constant efforts made by the researchers for effective degradation of POPs (persistent organic pollutants). The POPs are the group of organic chemical pollutants which have stable chemical structure, long persistence in nature, and retention ability in organisms. The degradation of these organic contaminants like hydrocarbons, dyes, halides and pesticides are urgent and serious issues because the contaminated water or food cause hazardous effects on human beings, wildlife and ecosystem [1–5]. Different approaches such as photocatalytic oxidation, adsorption, membrane filtration, ion-exchange, coagulation and biological treatment have been used for the decontamination of organic dyes from water [2–4]. Some of the techniques are inefficient because of the complex atomic structures of pollutants. In addition, some of the processes like adsorption, chemical precipitation, biological treatment, membrane filtration are not efficient for commercialization due to high cost, high chemical consumption, low removal capacity and generation of toxic by-products [6]. Among different techniques, photocatalysis is the most effective advanced oxidation method for treatment of wastewater containing dye molecules [7,8]. Photocatalysis converts the photon energy into chemical energy and dissociates the dyes into smaller molecules. However, there exists a challenge regarding the facile synthesis of stable and effective visible-light responsive photocatalysts.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) emerges as a promising alternatives in different field of applications like catalysis, photocatalysis, molecular adsorption, drug delivery, sensors [6,9]. They possess high surface area, tunable structure and chemistry via incorporation of different metal clusters and organic ligands. Variation in pore sizes allows the penetration of different inorganic/organic compounds. Among different MOFs, iron-based MOFs are gaining attraction because of abundant availability and cost-effectiveness. To date, numerous Fe-based MOFs like MIL-101(Fe) [10], MIL-88B [11], MIL-100(Fe) [12–15], MIL-68(Fe) [16], MIL-53(Fe) [17] have been used in the arena of photocatalysis because of their good response in presence of visible light and stimulation of H2O2 to •OH through a Fenton like method. Moreover, Fe-based MOFs have low toxicity, high chemical and water stabilities. However, their application was limited due to the low charge separation and effective excitons generation which, in turn, reduction in quantum yield and photocatalytic activity [18]. Additionally, due to the existence of voids and open framework of MOF, it is not able to retain small molecules that restricts the standalone application. To overcome these issues, a possible resolution lies in the combination of MOF with other substrates like semiconductors nanoparticles, metallic nanoparticles, graphene, reduced graphene oxide, activated carbon etc. This types of combination could tune the pore size of the MOF. In recent times, many research works [19–23] have revealed that graphene oxide has superior electron transport ability and visible light absorptivity leading to enhancement of the photogenerated charge carrier separation which, in turn, improve the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, incorporation of graphene oxide (GO) also improves the structural and photo stability of the photocatalyst [24]. From the literature, it is found that MOF with GO composites namely, MIL-88B(Fe)@GO [25], MIL-88A(Fe)@GO [19], MIL-101(Fe)/GO[26],MIL-101(Fe)/CoFe2O4/GO [27] were efficient for photocatalytic degradation of organic water contaminants. Graphene quantum dot is a smaller fragment of reduced graphene oxide which contains maximum 10 sheets of graphene with dimension < 30 nm [28,29]. GQDs have attracted significant interest in a wide variety of applications because of their size, mechanical properties, photostability, non-toxicity, bio-compatibility which makes them superior compared to other semiconductor quantum dots and other fluorescent probes. However, the combination of MIL-100(Fe) with GQD as a photocatalyst for degradation of dyes has not been explored till date. In this regard, the current study focusses on the development of a new catalyst with improved photocatalytic properties by incorporation of highly conductive GQDs in MIL-100(Fe) framework. The degradation efficiency was assessed using Congo Red dye as the target contaminant under the effect of visible light.

The precursors for the synthesis of pure and composite photocatalyst i.e. graphite powder, iron powder and benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (BTC, 98 wt%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Sulphuric acid (98 wt%), orthophosphoric acid (88 wt%), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40 wt%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), potassium permanganate and ethanol (99.9%) were supplied by Merck, India. The target contaminant Congo red (CR) dye, an anionic dye with MW (molecular weight) of 696.66 g/mol was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Deionized water was used in all the experiments. All were the analytical grade reagents and used without any further purification.

The preparation of GQD involved hydrothermal reaction as reported previously [28]. Prior to the synthesis of GQD, GO was synthesized at temperature 50 °C by modified Hummer’s method as described by Das et al. [30]. GO was dispersed in H2O, followed by addition of 100 mM NaOH as a reducing agent. Hydrothermal treatment was performed at 200 °C for 24 h in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The GQD solution was collected by double phase separation processes. Firstly, centrifugation of solution at 1500 rpm to isolate the suspended particles followed by filtration with syringe filter (0.22 µm) to achieve the nanometer sized particles. Finally, sodium ions were separated by using dialysis bag (1 kDa MWCO) where the water was changed at 24 h interval.

MIL-100(Fe) was prepared by solvothermal reaction of Fe-powder, BTC,HNO3 (nitric acid), HF (hydrofluoric acid) and H2O as reported by Bhattacharjee et al. 2018 [31]. According to the reported study, iron powder (0.54 g) was mixed with BTC (1.35 g) followed by addition of HF (0.34 ml), HNO3 (0.26 ml) and water (48 ml). Then the mixture was sonicated for 1 h and transferred into an autoclave for reaction at 150 °C for 12 h. Centrifugation was performed to separate the brick red precipitate. The synthesized precipitate was purified by stirring 1g of MOF in 0.5 L of H2O at 90 °C for10h (water was changed after 5 h). This was followed by washing with 100 ml of ethanol at 80 °C for 10 h. The washed MIL-100(Fe) was dried in air oven at 150 °C for 12 h.

For preparing MIL-100(Fe)/GQD composites via hydrothermal method, GQD solution in water was mixed with the solution of iron powder (1 g) and BTC (2.5 g) followed by the addition of mixed acid (HF (0.68 ml) and HNO3(0.52 ml)) and water. The solution was sonicated for 1 h and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 12 hr. Similar protocol as MIL-100(Fe) was followed for the purification of the composite.

The synthesized samples were characterized in terms of their porosity, morphology, structure, and crystallinity. The crystal structure of samples was determined by utilizing X-ray Diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, D8 Advance) in the scanning range of 2θ = 5° to 40°. FTIR (Fourier transform infrared, 4000–400 cm−1 , SHIMADZU, IR Affinity 1, Japan) was used to investigate the functional groups in MIL and MIL/GQD composite. N2 adsorption desorption study was performed to examine the mesopore and micropore surface area, pore volume and pore size. The surface chemistry of the materials was characterized by XPS (PHI 5000 Versa Probe III, M/s Physical electronics, USA) analysis. The morphology of the samples was visualized by using field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss, Sigma) and field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, JEM-2100F). Further, photoelectrochemical properties were recorded by using interface potentiostat (Model: 1010E, Gamry Instruments).

Fig. 1 shows the XRD, FTIR and N2 adsorption desorption study of parent MOF and GQD decorated composite samples. The structure and crystallographic features of MIL-100(Fe) and composite were analyzed by XRD. As depicted in Fig. 1a, the diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ of 5.3°, 11°, 14.2°, 18.2°, 20.1°, 24° and 27.7° which are in compliance with the crystallographic planes of (333), (428), (088), (7911), (4814), (6618) and (9321), respectively. The XRD pattern of the prepared MOF is found to be similar with the reported form of MIL-100(Fe) [32]. Additionally, the position of peaks of the MIL-100(Fe)/GQD composites are similar to MIL-100(Fe) with slight reduction in intensity. This observation confirms that the structural integrity of characteristic MOF was conserved after formation of composite MIL-100(Fe)/GQD by in-situ synthesis method. The FTIR spectra of pure MIL-100(Fe) and composite samples are represented in Fig. 1b. In FTIR spectra, the vibrational bands at 708, 757, 1383 and 1443 cm−1 are observed which are the distinguishing peaks of the MIL-100(Fe) [33]. Among them, bands at 708 and 757 cm−1 are correspond to the out of plane bending mode of C-H coming from phenyl group, while bands at 1383 and 1443 cm−1 are conforming to the C—O and C=O, respectively [34–36]. The band at 628 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of Fe-O. A weak signal noticed between 1710 and 1720 cm−1 signifies the presence of residual trimesic acid inside the porous structure. Band at ∼ 3500 cm−1 denotes the stretching vibration of O—H arising from an unreacted carboxylic acid group. After modification with GQD, the representative four peaks of MIL-100(Fe) remain unchanged, whereas band intensity of C—O is increased. This observation indicated that GQD is well combined with MIL-100(Fe) via formation of C—O bonds [37]. Further, the FTIR and XRD of the GQD are represented in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). A broad peak was found at 3325 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of O–H and the presence of CC was reflected by the peak at 1639 cm−1. Surface area and porosity of the nanoparticles were analyzed by N2 adsorption desorption study. Fig. 1c and d represents the adsorption isotherm and pore size distribution plot of pure and composite materials. Both materials follow Type I isotherm representing the micropores. This finding implies that the pore geometry and pore size of the material are not changed noticeably after formation of composite. The pristine MIL-100(Fe) possessed pore volume and specific surface area (SA) of 0.883 cc/g and 1804.4 m2/g (micropore SA: 1473 m2/g and mesopore SA: 331.2 m2/g), respectively. The pore volume and specific surface area slightly increased to 0.889 cc/g and 1878 m2/g in the composite sample, respectively. In the composite sample, micropore SA increased to 1592 m2/g and mesopore SA decreased to 285.8 m2/g. This changes in surface area and pore volume may be due to the increased development of micropores and reduction of mesopores in the composite [38,39]. Pore diameter of the pure and composite samples are found to be similar.

Further, XPS analysis was done to check the specific elements and composition of composites. The full scan XPS spectrum of composite and parent sample are shown in Fig. 2 a and b. Major peaks are observed at 285.3, 532.4, 712.1 and 725.7 eV which corresponds to the typical peaks of C1s, O1s, Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2, respectively. O Auger signal is observed at 976 eV in full scan XPS spectrum [40,41]. The high resolution spectrum of Fe2p (Fig. 2c and d) shows no changes in the composition of MIl-100(Fe) after formation of composite. Fig. 2e and 2f represent the O1s elemental spectra of pristine MOF and GQD decorated composite materials. Deconvolution O1s (Fig. 2e and f) contributes two peaks at 533 and 531.7 eV which are corresponding to the surface hydroxyl groups and presence of oxygen vacancies, respectively [38]. Area under the curve of the surface hydroxyl group of composite is found to be similar with that of pure materials. However, the area under the curve of oxygen vacancies of the composite materials is higher than pure MIL-100(Fe). This finding represents higher photodegradation efficiency of the composite materials. Fig. 2g and 2 h represent the high resolution C1s spectrum of MIL-100(Fe) and composite samples, respectively. Four different carbon peaks are found after deconvolution of C1s peak. The peaks are observed at 284.5, 285.1, 286.4 and 288.8 eV which represented the peaks of C=C, C–C, C–O and C=O, respectively. It is noteworthy that peak area of C=C is found to be higher for composite material than parent material. This is due to the fact that GQD is composed of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [42]. This study also confirms the successful formation of GQD incorporated composite material.

FESEM and TEM images of the prepared samples are represented in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a represents the flake-like morphology of GO. The regular cubic octahedral structure with even surface, sharp edges and uniform crystal phase are observed in Fig. 3c which is consistent with the literature of MIL-100(Fe) structure. It is clearly visible from Fig. 3f that the surface of the Fe-MOF is rough and crack after modification with GQD. This observation is clearer from the high magnification image (Fig. 3g) of composite sample. Similar observation was noticed from the corresponding TEM images (Fig. 3). FESEM and TEM studies also demonstrated that GQD does not affect morphology of MIL-100(Fe) after composite formation.

The catalytic activity of MIL-100(Fe)/GQD composites was studied by decolorization of Congo-red dye in the presence of visible light. The catalytic activity experiments were done in a photoreactor (pyrex glass) furnished with a water circulating jacket, molecular oxygen opening and a magnetic bar. LED lamp (400 W) was utilized for irradiation. Experiment has been carried out by varying dose concentration of composites to optimize the catalyst dosage. Congo red dye solution (50 ppm) with 0.025 g L−1 synthesized nanocomposites were stirred for 30 min in the dark for 30 min under ambient conditions to acquire Congo-red and composite surface adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, 5 ml sample was taken out before starting of irradiation to calculate the initial dye concentration. 5 ml of samples were collected at specific intervals and the absorbance values were recorded. UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV–Vis 1601) was used to track the absorption related decolorization of Congo-red. The concentration of dye was evaluated from a standard calibration curve. Effect of solution pH (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12), photocatalyst dosage, and initial dye concentration on degradation efficiency have been studied. The reusability and recyclability test of the nanocomposite as a catalyst was studied by using the same batch of photocatalysts for four cycles.

Fig. 4 a represents the UV–Vis DRS spectrum of the synthesized pure and composite materials. The absorption of two samples are positioned in the region of visible light. The spectrum of pure and composite photocatalysts displayed the combination of two spectra with absorption edges at 522 and 653 nm corresponding to the pure MIL-100(Fe) and 511 and 639 nm for MIL-100(Fe)/GQD. The band gap energy of the MIL-Fe(100)/GQD was calculated from the UV–vis absorption spectrum by using the equation (1):
(1)
( α h υ )
2
= B
( h υ -
E g
)
where Eg is the absorption band gap. α, B and υ are the coefficient of absorption, proportionality constant and frequency of photons, respectively. The plot of (αhυ)2 against hυ is shown in Fig. 4b. The band gap of the synthesized composite nanocatalysts are calculated from the plot of Fig. 4b. The values of Eg for the pure and composite photocatalysts are valued to be 2.35 and 1.93 eV, respectively. The synthesized composite has lower band gap energy which shows better catalytic activity than pristine MIL-100(Fe).

PL spectra were utilized for determination of the charge separation and transfer capacity of photogenerated e-/h+ pairs. The lower PL intensity is the description of the lower photo induced charge carrier recombination. Fig. 5 shows the PL spectra of MIL-100(Fe)/GQD and MIL-100(Fe) for excitation wavelength 320 nm. The main emission peaks appear at around 435 nm appear for all the photocatalysts and the intensity of emission is found to be higher for composite samples. This result signified that the combination of MIL-100(Fe) with GQD attributed to the larger separation of e-/h+ pairs, which in turns, exhibits higher degradation efficiency for composite materials. Graphene and graphene quantum dots possess high electrical conductivity. The addition of GQDs facilitates the transport of electrons, hence lowering the rate of e-/h+ recombination [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time on degradation of CR
ce:section.ce:para: The degradation of Congo red (CR) by the synthesized photocatalyst was studied using UV–Vis spectra at different time. The self-degradation of the dye was found to be negligible in the absence of a photocatalyst as shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information). The consequence of contact time on photocatalytic decolorization of CR is represented in Fig. 6 . The percentage degradation was enhanced with increasing the time of contact and attained equilibrium within 30 min. Removal percentage is found to be rapid within initial 5 mins of contact time for all samples. The higher degradation at the start of the reaction was observed because higher numbers of surface active sites are accessible for degradation of the dye during the initial stage. The activity of the photocatalyst became slowly reduced with time as the few surface active sites are available for reaction and difficult to occupy in the residual vacant sites because of the repulsive forces between the solute molecules on the solid and bulk phases. It is worth noting that MIL-100(Fe)/GQD demonstrated higher removal efficiency than pure MIL-100(Fe). This result is obvious because composite sample exhibits higher BET surface area, higher band gap energy and lower PL intensity than parent MOF. Furthermore, other studies were performed by using the composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQD on degradation of CR
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of GQD on degradation, four different composites were synthesized by mixing GQD solution (5 ml, 7 ml, 9 ml and 12 ml) with 1 g of iron powder. Then equal amounts of all four synthesized composite materials have been used for degradation study. Further, the degradation study was performed for 30 mins in presence of visible light. Fig. 7 a, represents the plot of effect of GQD on degradation of CR. It can be noted from the plot that the degradation of CR increases with the increasing amount of GQD. However, the increase in degradation for composite containing 12 ml GQD is not noticeably improved over 9 ml GQD based composite. Therefore, 9 ml GQD contained composite can be considered as an optimum concentration.

The photocatalytic removal of the dye (initial concentration of 50 mg/L) at various pH has been plotted in Fig. 7b. There is an increase in the removal from 89% to 93% upon increasing pH from 2 to 6. However, further increase in pH to 12 results in drastic reduction of the degradation. At higher pH the MOF/GQD composite becomes negatively charged [44]. Hence, repulsion occurs between negatively charge dye and the photocatalyst. Hence, pH 7 was optimized for further studies based on pH of natural water as well as higher dye degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of photocatalyst concentration on degradation of CR
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalyst concentration has a significant impact on photodegradation of dye. For our experiment, the photocatalyst dose was varied from 0.01 g/L to 0.1 g/L. Maximum removal was obtained at 0.025 g/L of the photocatalyst. The percentage degradation of dye enhances with increasing the amount of catalyst from 0.01 to 0.025 g/L, which is shown in Fig. 7c. This is because of the fact that the greater number of surface active sites of photocatalyst are present for adsorption of dye by increasing catalyst concentration. So, generation of •OH and superoxide radicals are increased which are used for the actual discoloration of dye solution. However, for 0.1 g/L photocatalyst, the removal reduces significantly. This can be ascribed to the solution becoming cloudy and accumulation of catalyst particles when concentration of the photocatalyst crosses the certain limit. As a result, decolorization efficiency of the photocatalyst decreases due to the blockage of the UV radiation and/or unavailability of the part of the catalyst surface for the photons absorption. Therefore, the optimum concentration of catalyst is important to get maximum degradation efficiency and is chosen as 0.025 g/L for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dye concentration on degradation of CR
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption of Congo-red on the photocatalyst surface is used for the photodegradation process. The quantity of the dye adsorbed on the catalyst surface only contributes to the photocatalysis process, not the one in bulk of the solution. Therefore, initial dye concentration is important in each photocatalytic reaction for adsorption of dye. The dye concentration was varied from 10 to 200 mg/L based on concentration of dyes in textile wastewater. The percentage degradation decreases with increasing the concentration of dye at fixed dose of photocatalyst (0.025 g/L). This is due to the fact that more organic substances are adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface whereas a lower quantity of photons reach the surface of the photocatalyst. Accordingly, lower •OH ions are generated and the decolorization percentage is found to be low. Fig. 7d demonstrates the photo degradation of congo red, depicting that the percentage photo degradation of Congo red decreases by increasing concentration of dye with fixed amount of photocatalyst. A sharp decrease in removal occurs on increasing concentration to 100 and 200 mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism for degradation of CR
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the MOFs matrix with high surface area and suitable pore size absorbs a certain amount of dye in dark, which offers the sufficient reaction space for the further photocatalysis process. Secondly, the GQD impregnated MOF reduces the band gap energy of MOF (2.35 eV → 1.93 eV), thus an improvement of photocatalytic activity via formation of oxygen vacancies (confirmed from XPS) in the composite material[45]. Under visible light irradiation, the valence band electrons MIL-100(Fe)/GQD are easily excited to the conduction band by visible light photons and forming holes (h+) in valence band. The possibility of e-/h+ recombination is decreased and hence more charge carriers are available to enhance the photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the trapping experiment of active species and Mott Schottky analysis have been performed to demonstrate the photocatalytic mechanism. The active species like h+ (holes), •O2 − (superoxide radical) and OH (hydroxyl radicals) are used for the catalytic degradation of organic contaminants in presence of light. To confirm the main active species formed over MIL-100(Fe)/GQD composites, it is essential to study the trapping experiments. In the detection, EDTA, t- butanol and ascorbic acid are utilized as the scavengers of OH (hydroxyl radical) and h+ (holes). Ascorbic acid is used as the scavenger of superoxide radical (̇O2 −). The photocatalytic degradation of Congo red over MIL-100/GQD was significantly inhibited by the addition of EDTA and t-butanol (Fig. 8 a). However, photocatalytic degradation was slightly decreased by the addition of ascorbic acid. From this study, it is confirmed that the •O2 − and h+ play vital roles in the catalytic degradation of contaminants. This finding is further confirmed from the Mott Schottky analysis. Incorporation of GQD on MIL-100(Fe) increased the concentration of electrons in the conduction band. The position of conduction band was estimated from Mott Schottky analysis (Fig. 8b). The flat band potential which is also termed as conduction band position (ECB) or fermi level was found to be −0.375 V and −0.668 V vs Ag/AgCl for pure and composite samples, respectively which relates to −0.18 V and −0.471 V vs NHE. A cathodic shift of flat band potential towards negative value after formation of composite explains the lower probability of e-/h+ pairs recombination, thus leading to increased photoreaction rates. The position of valence band was calculated from the equation: Eg = EVB-ECB. The valence band (VB) positioned was found to be 2.17 V and 1.42 V vs NHE, respectively for pure and composite materials. The position of conduction band and valence band are represented in Fig. 8c. Based on the analysis of band structure of MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100(Fe)/GQD, photogenerated electron and holes were transferred through the Z-scheme category [46]. Moreover, the photogenerated electrons will accumulate in the CB of MIL-100(Fe) and produce •O2 − radical (O2/•O2 − = −0.046 V vs. NHE) via reduction of O2. The VB of pure and composite materials exhibited a lower potential (2.17 and 1.82 V) than the reduction potential of •OH/OH− (2.38 V) or •OH/H2O (2.27 V). Therefore, the holes in composite were not sufficient to oxidize OH− to •OH, and h+ participated in the oxidation of pollutants directly. Therefore, •O2 − and h + were responsible for the degradation process.

It is important to examine the reusability and stability of the catalysts for real field applications. To study the stability performance, the XRD and FESEM (Figs. S3 and S4, SI) of the composite materials were performed after photocatalytic reaction. There are no noticeable peak shifting or morphological changes observed after degradation experiment. Further, a recycling experiment for degradation of Congo red has been carried out four times under the similar experimental conditions to study the reusability of the materials. After each cycle, photocatalyst was regenerated by centrifuging, washing with water and then drying at 60 °C for 12 h. The degradation rate of CR as shown in Fig. 9 decreased from 98.3 to 90.1% after four cycles. Fig. 9 depicted that the catalytic performance of MIL-100(Fe)/GQD did not change noticeably upto 4th consecutive cycles. Therefore, the synthesized photocatalyst shows excellent reusability and stability under visible light induced photocatalytic applications.

The photo degradation capacity of the photo-catalyst for the removal of Congo-red is found to be higher for MIL-100(Fe)/GQD compared to MIL-100(Fe). The removal efficiency of the as-synthesized composite has been compared with previously reported literature and presented in Table 1 .

In this research, pure MIL-100(Fe) and MIL-100 (Fe)/GQD composite photocatalysts were synthesized by an in-situ hydrothermal process and characterized by using different analytical tools. The synthesized photocatalysts were utilized for the degradation of Congo red dye. The composite MILs-100 (Fe)/GQD photocatalysts show excellent photocatalytic degradation in comparison with MIL-100(Fe). Based on the results of the different characterization technique, it is found that the wavelength of photo-absorption of MIL-100(Fe) is widened after modification with GQDs. GQDs are conducive to charge separation. The decolorization efficiency increases with enhancing the dose of photocatalyst and decreasing the concentration of dye. The photodegradation mechanism experiment confirms that the O2 − and h+ are the leading active species and play important roles in the decolorization of contaminants. The reusability test of the photocatalyst shows excellent stability and nominal degradation after four consecutive cycles. This studies confirm that modification of MIL-100(Fe) with GQD increases the visible light induced catalytic activity and stability of the photocatalyst; it may be a promising candidate for commercialization.

Debarati Mukherjee: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Pradip Das: Writing – original draft, Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sukanya Kundu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lal Mohan Kundu: . Bishnupada Mandal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the characterization facilities provided by the facility of the central instrument (CIF), Department of Chemistry, and Department of Chemical Engineering, Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Guwahati.

J. Chinese Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sohrabnezhad, Z. Kazemi, A. Pourahmad Nodehi, Synthesis and characterization of boehmite/metal – Organic framework of type AlO(OH)/MOF-74(Zn) for photocatalytic degradation of Congo red dye, J. Chinese Chem. Soc. 69 (2022) 939–949. doi: 10.1002/jccs.202200172.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategic combination of metal–organic frameworks and C3N4 for expeditious photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants
Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.P. Kumar, M. Ahmad, M.A. Nazir, A. Anum, M. Jamshaid, S.S.A. Shah, A. Rehman, Strategic combination of metal–organic frameworks and C3N4 for expeditious photocatalytic degradation of dye pollutants, Environ. Sci. Pollut. Res. 29 (2022) 35300–35313. doi: 10.1007/s11356-021-17366-w.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3O4 nanoparticles decorated with UIO-66 metal-organic framework particles and encapsulated in a triazine-based covalent organic framework matrix for photodegradation of anionic dyes
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zheng, C. Yao, Y. Xu, Fe3O4 Nanoparticles Decorated with UIO-66 Metal–Organic Framework Particles and Encapsulated in a Triazine-Based Covalent Organic Framework Matrix for Photodegradation of Anionic Dyes, ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 3 (2020) 11307–11314. doi: 10.1021/acsanm.0c02401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of Zn-MOFs by microwave-assisted ball milling for removal of tetracycline hydrochloride and Congo red from wastewater
Green Process. Synth.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Ren, F. Wei, H. Chen, D. Chen, B. Ding, Preparation of Zn-MOFs by microwave-assisted ball milling for removal of tetracycline hydrochloride and Congo red from wastewater, Green Process. Synth. 10 (2021) 125–133. doi: 10.1515/gps-2021-0020.
